questions.stem: A population is best defined as:

options.text: A group of interacting organisms of the same species living in an area
All the organisms of different species in an area
The non-living parts of an ecosystem

questions.explanation: A population is an interacting group of organisms of the same species living in an area.

questions.stem: What is used to distinguish one population of a species from another?
questions.explanation: Members of a population normally breed together; reproductive isolation distinguishes separate populations.

questions.stem: Why is population size usually estimated rather than counted exactly?

options.text: Counting every individual is often impractical
Individuals cannot be counted at all

questions.explanation: Estimating population size by sampling is used because counting every individual is usually impractical.

questions.scenario: Samples are taken without bias in choosing locations.

options.text: To avoid bias and make the estimate representative
To reduce the number of species

questions.explanation: Randomness in sampling avoids bias so the sample represents the whole population.

questions.scenario: An estimate of population size differs from the true size.
questions.explanation: Random sampling inevitably produces sampling error: the difference between the estimate and the true population size.

questions.scenario: A student estimates the number of a plant species in a field.
questions.explanation: Random quadrat sampling is used to estimate population size for sessile organisms such as plants.

questions.stem: Quadrat sampling is suitable for organisms that are:
questions.explanation: Random quadrat sampling works for sessile organisms (plants and sessile animals) that can be counted.

questions.scenario: The number of individuals per quadrat varies considerably.

options.text: The standard deviation of the mean

questions.explanation: The standard deviation of the mean number per quadrat measures variation and how evenly the population is spread.

questions.scenario: A student estimates a beetle population by catching, marking and releasing them, then recapturing.
questions.explanation: Capture-mark-release-recapture with the Lincoln index estimates population size for motile organisms.

questions.scenario: In the Lincoln index, 20 animals were marked, 25 recaptured, of which 5 were marked.
questions.stem: What is the estimated population size (M×N÷R)?
questions.explanation: Lincoln index = M×N÷R = 20×25÷5 = 100.

options.text: Maximum population size an environment can sustain
Number of species in a community

questions.explanation: Carrying capacity is the maximum population size that an environment's limited resources can support.

questions.stem: Which resource might limit the carrying capacity of a population?
questions.explanation: Limited resources such as food, water or space determine the carrying capacity through competition.

questions.scenario: As a population grows denser, disease spreads more easily and pushes numbers back down.
questions.stem: Disease acting in this way is a:
questions.explanation: Density-dependent factors, such as pathogen transfer, predation and competition, push a population back towards carrying capacity.

questions.scenario: A flood kills organisms regardless of how crowded the population is.
questions.explanation: Density-independent factors, like a flood, affect populations regardless of their density.

questions.stem: Density-dependent factors regulate population size by:

options.text: Negative feedback toward the carrying capacity

questions.explanation: Density-dependent factors act by negative feedback, pushing the population back toward the carrying capacity.

questions.scenario: In the early phase of a population growth curve, numbers rise very rapidly.
questions.explanation: The initial phase of sigmoid population growth is exponential, with resources abundant.

questions.scenario: A sigmoid (S-shaped) population growth curve is used to model growth.
questions.stem: According to NOS, this curve is:

options.text: An idealized model that simplifies a complex system
An exact record of every individual
A proven law with no exceptions

questions.explanation: The sigmoid growth curve is an idealized graphical model; models are often simplifications of complex systems.

questions.scenario: A student grows a population to model sigmoid growth.
questions.stem: Which organism is recommended for this?
questions.explanation: Yeast and duckweed are recommended because they proliferate quickly under experimental conditions.

questions.scenario: Members of the same species compete for the same food.
questions.explanation: Intraspecific competition is competition within a population, between members of the same species.

questions.scenario: Meerkats take turns watching for predators while others feed.
questions.stem: This within-species behaviour is an example of:
questions.explanation: Intraspecific relationships include both competition and cooperation, such as cooperative vigilance.

options.text: All the interacting organisms (all populations) in an ecosystem
Only the plants in an area

questions.explanation: A community is all of the interacting organisms in an ecosystem, including plants, animals, fungi and bacteria.

questions.scenario: One organism kills and eats another.
questions.explanation: Predation is an interspecific relationship in which one organism catches and eats another.

questions.scenario: An animal eats a plant.
questions.explanation: Herbivory is the interspecific relationship where an animal feeds on a plant.

questions.scenario: A tapeworm lives inside a host, harming it while benefiting itself.
questions.explanation: Parasitism benefits the parasite at the expense of the host, a category of interspecific relationship.

questions.scenario: Two species interact and both benefit.
questions.explanation: Mutualism is an interspecific relationship that benefits both species.

questions.scenario: Nitrogen-fixing bacteria live in root nodules of legumes (Fabaceae).
questions.explanation: Root nodules in Fabaceae are a mutualism: bacteria fix nitrogen for the plant, which supplies them with carbon compounds.

questions.scenario: Zooxanthellae live inside hard corals.
questions.explanation: Zooxanthellae in hard corals is a mutualism: the algae photosynthesize for the coral, which provides shelter and nutrients.

questions.scenario: Mycorrhizae associate with the roots of orchids (Orchidaceae).
questions.stem: This relationship is a form of:

options.text: Mutualism between fungi and plant roots

questions.explanation: Mycorrhizae in Orchidaceae are a mutualism between fungi and plant roots, benefiting both organisms.

questions.scenario: An introduced species outcompetes native species for resources.
questions.stem: The introduced species is described as:
questions.explanation: An introduced species that gains a competitive advantage over endemic species is invasive.

questions.stem: An endemic species is one that is:
questions.explanation: Endemic species are native to a particular area; invasive species are introduced and outcompete them.

questions.scenario: One species is more successful when a second species is absent.

options.text: Indicates but does not prove interspecific competition

questions.explanation: Greater success in the absence of another species indicates, but does not prove, interspecific competition.

questions.stem: Which is a valid approach to investigating interspecific competition?

options.text: Field manipulation by removing one species

questions.explanation: Interspecific competition can be studied by laboratory experiments, field observations and field manipulation such as removal.

questions.scenario: A student tests whether two species tend to occur together or apart across sampling sites.
questions.explanation: The chi-squared test can assess association between the presence/absence of two species across sampling sites.

questions.scenario: A chi-squared test shows two species rarely occur together.
questions.explanation: A negative association (species rarely together) may provide evidence for interspecific competition.

questions.scenario: As a prey population rises, the predator population later rises too, then prey numbers fall.

options.text: Density-dependent control by a predator-prey relationship

questions.explanation: Predator-prey relationships are an example of density-dependent control of animal populations.

questions.scenario: A population is controlled mainly by predators feeding on it.
questions.explanation: Top-down control occurs when predators (higher trophic levels) regulate a population; bottom-up control comes from resources.

questions.scenario: A population is limited mainly by the availability of nutrients for producers.
questions.explanation: Bottom-up control occurs when resource availability at lower trophic levels limits populations.

questions.scenario: A plant releases chemicals into the soil that inhibit the growth of nearby competing plants.
questions.explanation: Allelopathy is the release of a chemical into the environment to deter potential competitors.

questions.scenario: A fungus secretes a chemical that kills nearby bacteria.
questions.stem: This secretion of antibiotics is similar to allelopathy because both:

options.text: Release a chemical to deter competitors

questions.explanation: Allelopathy and antibiotic secretion both release a chemical into the environment to deter potential competitors.

questions.scenario: A population reaches a stable size where births roughly balance deaths.
questions.explanation: At the carrying capacity, the population stabilizes as limited resources balance growth.

questions.scenario: A student plots population size on a logarithmic scale against time on a normal scale.
questions.stem: A straight line on this graph indicates:
questions.explanation: Exponential growth appears as a straight line when population size is plotted on a logarithmic scale against time.

options.text: Are reproductively isolated from each other

questions.explanation: A population is an interbreeding group of the same species; reproductive isolation separates different populations.

questions.scenario: A bacterium causes disease in a plant.
questions.explanation: Pathogenicity, in which one organism causes disease in another, is a category of interspecific relationship.

questions.stem: Which is a reason for intraspecific competition?

options.text: Members of the same species need the same resources

questions.explanation: Intraspecific competition arises because members of the same species require the same limited resources.

questions.stem: Which is a density-dependent factor limiting population size?
questions.explanation: Density-dependent factors include competition, predation and disease; frosts, eruptions and floods are density-independent.

questions.stem: Hypotheses about competition can be tested by:
questions.explanation: Hypotheses can be tested by both experiments and observations, which differ in approach.

questions.stem: In a predator-prey relationship, a rise in prey numbers tends to be followed by:

options.text: The immediate extinction of the predator

questions.explanation: More prey supports more predators, whose numbers rise after the prey; this density-dependent cycling regulates populations.

questions.stem: Which of these would be included in a community?

options.text: Plants, animals, fungi and bacteria in the area

questions.explanation: A community comprises all the populations of organisms (plants, animals, fungi, bacteria) interacting in an area.

questions.stem: In a given community, top-down and bottom-up control:

options.text: Are both possible, but one is usually dominant

questions.explanation: Both types of control are possible, but one or the other is likely to be dominant in a particular community.

questions.stem: Increasing the number of samples taken generally:

options.text: Reduces sampling error and improves the estimate
Has no effect on the estimate

questions.explanation: More random samples generally reduce sampling error, giving a more reliable estimate of population size.
